text: Robotic gastrectomy due to chronic gastro pleural fistula secondary to leak from sleeve gastrectomy. We present a case of a twenty four year old female whose eleven months post a sleeve gastrectomy, complicated by a leak. Initially treated with TPN and stent placement. She now presents with fever and cough and has a recurrent leak into the left chest, pleural cavity, and into the left lung.
Plans were made for esophagogastrectomy using the robotic approach to deal with this chronic fistula.

The surgeon separates the stomach from the left lobe of the liver, identifying the right crus of the diaphragm. The sleeve is approached posteriorly and separated from surrounding inflammatory tissue near the angle of His and the left hemidiaphragm. The inflamed left hemidiaphragm is visible above, along with the inflammatory omentum and the fistula, which is identified using the suction tip. The opening at the sleeve esophageal junction is also noted.

The surgeon begins transection by using a linear stapler to take the left gastric vessels. The hiatus is freed, and the esophagus is dissected, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.

The surgeon continues dissection posterior to the sleeve at the gastroesophageal junction, moving towards the left side near the left hemidiaphragm and fistula. The sleeve is rotated medially, and the fistula is completely transected from the left hemidiaphragm.
The excellent three-D visualization afforded by the robotic technology allows this portion of the procedure to proceed with ease.

The surgeon identifies the left crus and opens the hiatus on the left side. The esophagus is freed and mobilized intra-abdominally, preparing for the esophago-jejunal anastomosis. Both right and left cruci are visible as the esophagus is moved intra-abdominally.

The surgeon transects the mid portion of the sleeve using a linear stapler. An opening is created in the proximal sleeve near the gastroesophageal junction to facilitate the passage of the anvil into the esophagus.
The surgeon passes the anvil into the distal esophagus and proceeds to transect the esophagus at its distal portion, carefully avoiding the anvil during the transection.

The specimen is removed from the surgical field and sent for pathological examination. The surgeon delivers the anvil through the distal esophagus, and prepares the small bowel for the esophago-jejunostomy anastomosis using a circular stapler.
The surgeon connects the anvil and circular stapler, closes them to perform the esophago-jejunal anastomosis, and then removes the circular stapler from the surgical site.

The surgeon reinforces the staple line adjacent to the esophago-jejunal anastomosis with interrupted sutures. Following the completion of the anastomosis, an endoscope is inserted to visually assess the integrity of the anastomosis and confirm the anatomical configuration.
The surgeon clamps the anastomosis and insufflates it with air while submerged underwater, observing no leakage of bubbles, indicating the integrity of the anastomosis.
